---
title: "Root Finding"
page-layout: full
---

<div class="hero">

<h1>Root Finding</h1>

<p>
Root finding is about solving equations of the form $f(x)=0$.
These problems appear throughout applied mathematics, scientific computing, statistics, engineering, and many other areas where exact algebraic solutions are not practical.
</p>

</div>

## Apps

<div class="card-grid">

<div class="topic-card">
<h3><a href="fixed-point.html">Fixed Point Iteration</a></h3>
<p>
Explore iterations of the form $x_{n+1}=g(x_n)$ using a cobweb diagram. Choose a function, pick a starting value, and advance the method one step at a time.
</p>
</div>

<div class="topic-card">
<h3><a href="newton.html">Newton's Method</a></h3>
<p>
Explore Newton's method through its tangent-line interpretation. Start from an initial guess and watch how each tangent line produces the next approximation.
</p>
</div>

</div>

<div class="explanation">

## Why root finding matters

A root of a function $f$ is a value $\alpha$ such that

$$
f(\alpha)=0.
$$

Root finding problems show up whenever we need to solve an equation that cannot be rearranged easily by hand. For example, roots appear in nonlinear modeling, optimization, numerical integration, differential equations, statistics, and simulation.

In this section, the goal is not just to compute an answer. The goal is to see how the methods move from one approximation to the next.

## Methods in this section

The first app studies fixed point iteration:

$$
x_{n+1}=g(x_n).
$$

This method is especially useful for understanding convergence geometrically, because the iteration can be drawn using the graphs of $y=g(x)$ and $y=x$.

The second app studies Newton's method:

$$
x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}.
$$

Newton's method uses tangent lines to build successive approximations to a root. When it works well, it can converge very quickly, but the visualization also helps show why it can fail for poor starting points or nearly flat derivatives.

</div>
